Patch Set 6:

Starting to look at lustre_rmmod,I'm wondering why the script as a whole can't go away. The script appears to do exactly what modprobe is meant to do. If you want to remove all lustre modules, that would simply be a 'modprobe -r libcfs' (since I think that's the lowest module in the stack). It would remove a maintenance burden as well.

Looking at the manpages online for Ubuntu, Suse, Fedora, and RHEL, it appears that 'modprobe -r' has been supported for a while.

This patch also lays down some ground work for other systemd services to be written to help ensure a clean shutdown.

What's the correct way to unabandon a change that is not my own? I was just going to resubmit the patch from 5478 as a new change.

looking at the script, I agree with you. I'll add that change.

Patch Set 9: Code-Review+1

(1 comment)

Patch Set 9:

> (1 comment)

I could certainly do that, but I'm inclined to say that once we switch to the /sys/ equivalent, then all references to '/proc/sys/lnet' should change at that time. There is no sense in leaving '/proc/sys/lnet' laying around in scripts, etc if it is unused.

parents.subject: LU-9415 lfsck: quiet noisy console message

commit.subject: LU-9439 scripts: lnet systemd service
commit.message:
LU-9439 scripts: lnet systemd service

Create an lnet systemd service which properly
brings lnet up and down.

Test-Parameters: trivial
Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Di Natale <dinatale2@llnl.gov>
Change-Id: I584827dbb1fc4e0999b7f107bb4250678b7b68e8
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/26925
Tested-by: Jenkins
Reviewed-by: Ned Bass <bass6@llnl.gov>
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
